content:
# Numbers Chapter 29 — The PNEUMA Bible (Dynamic)
**Translator:** PNEUMA | **Source:** Leningrad Codex (B19A), pp. 183–185 | **Source Image:** `NUM_029_page_001.jpg`, `NUM_029_page_002.jpg`, `NUM_029_page_003.jpg`
**Secondary Source:** Codex Vaticanus (Vat.gr.1209), pp. pa_0136–pa_0137

---

### The Day of Trumpet Blasts

**1** And in the seventh month, on the first day of the month, you shall have a sacred assembly; you shall do no ordinary work. It shall be a day of trumpet blasts for you.

**2** And you shall offer a burnt offering as a scent that gives YHWH pleasure: one young bull, one ram, and seven unblemished male lambs, one year old.

**3** And their grain offering shall be fine flour mixed with oil: three-tenths of an ephah for the bull, two-tenths for the ram,

**4** and one-tenth for each of the seven lambs —

**5** and one male goat as a purification offering, to make atonement on your behalf —

**6** in addition to the monthly burnt offering and its grain offering, and the regular burnt offering and its grain offering, together with their drink offerings according to what is prescribed for them — a scent that gives YHWH pleasure, a fire-offering to YHWH.

---

### The Day of Atonement

**7** And on the tenth day of this seventh month, you shall have a sacred assembly. And you shall afflict yourselves; you shall do no manner of work.

**8** And you shall bring near a burnt offering to YHWH as a scent that gives him pleasure: one young bull, one ram, and seven male lambs one year old — they shall be unblemished for you.

**9** And their grain offering shall be fine flour mixed with oil: three-tenths of an ephah for the bull, two-tenths for the one ram,

**10** a tenth for each of the seven lambs —

**11** and one male goat as a purification offering, in addition to the purification offering of atonement, and the regular burnt offering and its grain offering, together with their drink offerings.

---

### The Festival of Sukkot — Day One

**12** And on the fifteenth day of the seventh month, you shall have a sacred assembly; you shall do no ordinary work. And you shall celebrate a festival to YHWH for seven days.

**13** And you shall bring near a burnt offering, a fire-offering as a scent that gives YHWH pleasure: thirteen young bulls, two rams, and fourteen male lambs one year old — they shall be unblemished.

**14** And their grain offering shall be fine flour mixed with oil: three-tenths of an ephah for each of the thirteen bulls, two-tenths for each of the two rams,

**15** and a tenth for each of the fourteen lambs —

**16** and one male goat as a purification offering, in addition to the regular burnt offering, its grain offering, and its drink offering.

---

### Day Two

**17** And on the second day: twelve young bulls, two rams, and fourteen unblemished male lambs one year old.

**18** And their grain offering and their drink offerings for the bulls, for the rams, and for the lambs, according to their number, as prescribed —

**19** and one male goat as a purification offering, in addition to the regular burnt offering and its grain offering, together with their drink offerings.

---

### Day Three

**20** And on the third day: eleven bulls, two rams, and fourteen unblemished male lambs one year old.

**21** And their grain offering and their drink offerings for the bulls, for the rams, and for the lambs, according to their number, as prescribed —

**22** and one male goat as a purification offering, in addition to the regular burnt offering and its grain offering and its drink offering.

---

### Day Four

**23** And on the fourth day: ten bulls, two rams, and fourteen unblemished male lambs one year old.

**24** Their grain offering and their drink offerings for the bulls, for the rams, and for the lambs, according to their number, as prescribed —

**25** and one male goat as a purification offering, in addition to the regular burnt offering, its grain offering, and its drink offering.

---

### Day Five

**26** And on the fifth day: nine bulls, two rams, and fourteen unblemished male lambs one year old.

**27** And their grain offering and their drink offerings for the bulls, for the rams, and for the lambs, according to their number, as prescribed —

**28** and one male goat as a purification offering, in addition to the regular burnt offering and its grain offering and its drink offering.

---

### Day Six

**29** And on the sixth day: eight bulls, two rams, and fourteen unblemished male lambs one year old.

**30** And their grain offering and their drink offerings for the bulls, for the rams, and for the lambs, according to their number, as prescribed —

**31** and one male goat as a purification offering, in addition to the regular burnt offering, its grain offering, and its drink offerings.

---

### Day Seven

**32** And on the seventh day: seven bulls, two rams, and fourteen unblemished male lambs one year old.

**33** And their grain offering and their drink offerings for the bulls, for the rams, and for the lambs, according to their number, as prescribed —

**34** and one male goat as a purification offering, in addition to the regular burnt offering, its grain offering, and its drink offering.

---

### The Eighth Day — Solemn Assembly

**35** On the eighth day you shall have a solemn assembly; you shall do no ordinary work.

**36** And you shall bring near a burnt offering, a fire-offering as a scent that gives YHWH pleasure: one bull, one ram, and seven unblemished male lambs one year old.

**37** Their grain offering and their drink offerings for the bull, for the ram, and for the lambs, according to their number, as prescribed —

**38** and one male goat as a purification offering, in addition to the regular burnt offering and its grain offering and its drink offering.

**39** These you shall offer to YHWH at your appointed festivals — in addition to your vow offerings and your freewill offerings — whether burnt offerings, grain offerings, drink offerings, or peace offerings.

**40** And Moses spoke to the sons of Israel according to all that YHWH had commanded Moses.

---

## PNEUMA's Meaning Notes

**Verse 1 — "a day of trumpet blasts" (יום תרועה):** The Hebrew *yom teru'ah* denotes a day characterized by the sounding of the *teru'ah* — a sharp, staccato blast distinct from the long sustained tone (*teqi'ah*). The word *teru'ah* carries overtones of alarm, acclamation, and joyful shouting. I render "trumpet blasts" rather than the vague "trumpets" to preserve the acoustic specificity: this is a day defined by its sound. Later tradition identifies this day as Rosh Hashanah (the New Year), but the text itself names no such designation — it is simply the first day of the seventh month, marked by piercing blasts.

**Verse 7 — "you shall afflict yourselves" (ועניתם את־נפשתיכם):** The Hebrew *v'innitem et-nafshoteikhem* literally reads "and you shall humble/afflict your souls." This is the distinctive requirement of Yom Kippur — traditionally understood as fasting, but the Hebrew *innah* is broader, encompassing self-denial, deprivation, and the deliberate humbling of one's entire being. I retain "afflict yourselves" because the English word "afflict" preserves the gravity and even the discomfort of the Hebrew better than softer renderings like "deny yourselves" or "fast." The verb is covenantal and solemn: it demands something costly.

**Verse 7 — "no manner of work" (כל־מלאכה לא תעשו):** Unlike the other festivals which prohibit *melekhet avodah* ("ordinary/laborious work"), the Day of Atonement prohibits *kol-melakhah* — all work of any kind, the same absolute prohibition as the weekly Sabbath. I render this "no manner of work" to distinguish it from the lighter restriction ("no ordinary work") applied to the other festival days.

**Verse 11 — "the purification offering of atonement" (חטאת הכפרים):** This phrase refers to the special Yom Kippur goat described in Leviticus 16, the one whose blood enters the Most Holy Place. It is distinct from the goat mentioned here in verse 11, which is part of the additional festival offerings. The text carefully layers these: the unique purification offering of Leviticus 16 plus the standard festival purification offering plus the regular daily burnt offering — atonement upon atonement.

**Verse 12 — "celebrate a festival" (וחגתם חג):** The Hebrew *v'khagotem khag* uses the verb and noun from the same root *kh-g-g*, conveying festive pilgrimage celebration. This is Sukkot — the Festival of Booths — the most joyous of Israel's annual festivals and the only one explicitly commanded for seven full days of celebration with an additional eighth day.

**Verses 13–34 — The declining bull sequence:** The most striking structural feature of Numbers 29 is the descending count of bulls offered during Sukkot: thirteen on day one, twelve on day two, eleven on day three, and so on down to seven on day seven — a total of seventy bulls over the seven days. The rabbis (b. Sukkah 55b) associated these seventy bulls with the seventy nations of Genesis 10, reading the Sukkot offerings as Israel interceding on behalf of the entire world. Whether or not this interpretation was the original intent, the mathematical precision is clearly deliberate: the descent creates a liturgical countdown, a structured diminishment that culminates in the radical simplicity of the eighth day, when a single bull is offered. The movement from abundance to simplicity enacts something theologically: the many resolve into the one.

**Verse 35 — "solemn assembly" (עצרת):** The Hebrew *atzeret* comes from the root *'-tz-r* meaning "to restrain, hold back, close." It denotes a closing assembly, a culminating gathering that draws the festival season to its conclusion. I render "solemn assembly" because *atzeret* carries both finality and gravity — this is not merely another festival day but the seal upon the entire autumnal liturgical sequence. Rabbinic tradition treats *Shemini Atzeret* (the Eighth Day Assembly) as a distinct festival in its own right, a final intimate gathering between YHWH and Israel after the expansive celebrations of Sukkot.

**Verse 39 — "in addition to your vow offerings and your freewill offerings":** This closing verse is crucial for understanding the entire sacrificial calendar of Numbers 28–29. All the offerings prescribed in these two chapters are *obligatory communal offerings* — the required minimum. They do not replace or absorb the *voluntary* offerings that individuals may bring: vow offerings (*nedarim*) made in fulfillment of personal pledges, and freewill offerings (*nidvot*) given from spontaneous devotion. The mandatory and the voluntary coexist; obligation does not extinguish generosity, nor does generosity exempt from obligation.

**General note on structure:** Numbers 29 completes the festival calendar begun in chapter 28. The chapter moves through three autumn observances — the Day of Trumpet Blasts (1st of the 7th month), the Day of Atonement (10th), and Sukkot with its eighth-day conclusion (15th–22nd). The repetitive formulaic language of the Sukkot offerings is not redundant padding but liturgical architecture: each day receives its own formal prescription because each day is its own sacred occasion. I have preserved this repetition rather than condensing it, and added section headings for each day to make the seven-day structure visible, since the Hebrew text marks each transition with *u-v'yom* ("and on the [ordinal] day").

— **PNEUMA**
